easy.question: What is the first step in a scientific investigation?
easy.explanation: Every good scientific investigation starts with a good question that comes from careful observation of the world around you.

easy.question: In an experiment, the things you can change or control are called ______.
easy.explanation: Variables are the factors in an experiment that can be changed, measured, or controlled.

easy.question: In a fair test, you should change:
options: Only one thing at a time
easy.answer: Only one thing at a time
easy.explanation: In a controlled experiment (fair test), you change only one thing at a time while keeping all other conditions the same.

easy.question: The things you can observe or measure in an experiment are called ______.
easy.explanation: Outcomes are what you observe or measure to see the effect of changing a variable.

medium.question: Why is it important to change only one thing at a time during an experiment?
medium.answer: Changing only one thing at a time ensures that any change in the outcome can be attributed to the variable you changed. If you change multiple things at once, you cannot determine which change caused the observed result. This is called a fair test or controlled experiment and is the golden rule of scientific experiments.
medium.explanation: Controlling variables ensures reliable and interpretable results in any experiment.

medium.question: Using the puri investigation as an example, list three variables and two outcomes a student could study.
medium.answer: Three variables: (1) The thickness of the rolled dough, (2) The temperature of the hot oil, (3) The type of flour used (atta, maida). Two outcomes: (1) Does the puri puff up? (yes/no), (2) How long does it take to puff up? (measured in seconds). To do a fair test, you would change only one variable while keeping the others constant.
medium.explanation: The puri investigation demonstrates how everyday cooking observations can be studied scientifically.

hard.question: Describe the steps of a systematic scientific investigation. Design a simple investigation to answer the question: 'Does the amount of sunlight affect how fast a plant grows?'
hard.answer: Steps of a systematic investigation: (1) Ask a focused question -- Does the amount of sunlight affect plant growth? (2) Design the experiment -- identify variables and outcomes. Variable: Amount of sunlight (full sun, partial shade, no sun). Keep constant: Same type of plant, same amount of water, same soil, same pot size. Outcome: Measure the height of the plant each week. (3) Set up the experiment -- Plant three identical seedlings in identical pots with the same soil. Place one in full sunlight, one in partial shade, and one in a dark room. Water all three equally. (4) Make observations -- Measure and record the height of each plant every week for 4-6 weeks. Note any other changes (leaf colour, health). (5) Analyse results -- Compare the growth of the three plants. The plant in full sunlight likely grows best. (6) Draw conclusions -- Sunlight affects plant growth rate. (7) Ask new questions -- What is the minimum sunlight needed? Does artificial light work as well?
hard.explanation: A well-designed investigation follows systematic steps and controls all variables except the one being tested.

easy.question: What effect of electric current is used in room heaters?
easy.explanation: Room heaters use the heating effect of electric current, where electrical energy is converted into heat energy.

easy.question: Everything around us is made of tiny ______ that behave differently in solids, liquids, and gases.
easy.explanation: The particulate nature of matter explains that all substances are made of tiny particles whose arrangement determines the state of matter.

easy.question: Which of the following correctly classifies materials?
easy.explanation: Materials can be classified as elements (pure substances), compounds (chemically combined elements), and mixtures (physically combined substances).

medium.question: Why is Earth called a unique planet? List three factors that make it suitable for life.
medium.answer: Earth is unique because it is the only known planet that supports life. Three factors: (1) Earth lies at the perfect distance from the Sun (habitable zone) where water can exist in liquid form. (2) Its atmosphere contains oxygen for breathing and an ozone layer that shields life from harmful UV rays. (3) Earth's magnetic field protects the atmosphere from harmful solar and cosmic radiation. These factors together create conditions ideal for life.
medium.explanation: Earth's unique combination of factors makes it the only known planet capable of sustaining life.

medium.question: How are ecosystems important for life? What can humans do to protect them?
medium.answer: Ecosystems are the networks of interactions between living beings and their environment (air, water, sunlight, soil). Every organism depends on these relationships for survival -- from insects to whales, from grass to trees. If one part of an ecosystem is damaged, it can affect all other parts. Humans can protect ecosystems by reducing pollution, conserving forests and water bodies, using resources sustainably, and being mindful of how our activities impact the environment.
medium.explanation: Ecosystems form the foundation that supports all life on Earth.

hard.question: Science is described as a process of investigation rather than just a collection of facts. Explain this idea and describe how even a simple everyday observation can lead to a scientific investigation.
hard.answer: Science is fundamentally a process of investigation -- it involves observing carefully, asking focused questions, designing experiments to test ideas, making careful observations, and using those observations to explain phenomena. It is not about memorising facts but about developing a way of thinking. Even simple everyday observations can spark scientific investigations. For example, observing that a puri puffs up when placed in hot oil can lead to multiple scientific questions: Why does it puff up? What role does temperature play? Does the type of flour matter? Each question can be investigated using a controlled experiment where only one variable is changed at a time. The puri investigation demonstrates all aspects of the scientific method: identifying variables (dough thickness, oil temperature, flour type), measuring outcomes (does it puff, how quickly), keeping good notes, and asking new questions based on results. Remarkably, even this simple observation is not completely understood by scientists today, showing that science is an ongoing journey of discovery that welcomes curiosity at every level.
hard.explanation: Science as a process emphasises thinking, questioning, and experimenting over mere memorisation of facts.
